english: if actually done, and in legends, especially, it is generally followed by a happy realisatiou of what had been desired. — dmigs-so (dmigs so) S.O. prob.: it is imaginable, it may be done in mind; don dmigs-pa (don dmigs pa) to intend a benefit or profit for another person Mil. — Generally 2. sbst., thought, idea, fancy, vulgo *mig(s)* (mig(s)); dmigs-pai rten (dmigs pai rten) prob.: a thing only supposed, an object imagined Thgr.; dmigs-pa źig ston-pa (dmigs pa zhig ston pa), bógs-pa (bogs pa) to give (to another person) an idea of, to make a suggestion Mil.; *mig-la čo go* (mig la co go) W. means also: do it, execute it, according to your own mind, I cannot supply you an exact pattern of it; dmigs-ćan (dmigs can) ingenious, skilful in contriving W.; dmigs-pa-las dás-pa (dmigs pa las 'das pa) = bsám-byai yul-las dás-pa (bsam byai yul las 'das pa) ? — yéńs-med(-par) dmigs-pa (dań) brál-bas-na (yengs med (-par) dmigs pa (dang) bral bas na) indisturbable by fancies of the mind, free from every working of the imagination Mil.; dmigs-pa-méd-pai sñiń-rǰe (dmigs pa med pai snying rje) Mil. seems to be, acc. to Thgy., the pity which the accomplished saint, who has found every thing, even religion, to be vain and empty, feels towards all other beings, in as far as they are still subject to error and mistake, opp. to séms-ćan-la dmigs-pai sñiń-rǰe (sems can la dmigs pai snying rje), and čós-la dmigs-pai sñiń-rǰe (chos la dmigs pai snying rje) the tender sympathies called forth by the sight of beings that are really suffering and of those defective in morality — a play upon empty phrases, in as much as in the very narrative, from which the passage above is quoted, the natural softness of Milaraspa is evidently excited by a very positive case, and not by any reflexions of an abstract nature. — *mig-pa-nę zó-pa* (mig pa ne zo pa) (v. bzó-ba (bzo ba)) C. done only in thought, supposed, fictitious; dmigs ťams-ćád brǰéd-nas (dmigs thams cad brjed nas) forgetful of all the beautiful fancies, schemes, and airy notions; dmigs-pa γtód-pa (dmigs pa gtod pa) prob.: to direct one's thoughts, fancies, la (la) to Tar. 189, 2. (where, no doubt, γtád-na (gtad na) is to be read); dmigs-γtád (dmigs gtad) mental object, dmigs-γtád brál-bai rnál-°byor-pa (dmigs gtad bral bai rnal 'byor pa) a saint that is free from such objects; acc. to our Lama also = γtád-so (gtad so) q.v.; dmigs-γsál (dmigs gsal) Lex.; (Sch.: 'a clear notion'), perh. misspelt for dmigs-bsál (dmigs bsal) exception from a rule Gram.; a particular mention, marking out, exemption of a person, in magisterial orders or enactments W. — dmigs-bu (dmigs bu) a blind man's leader Dzl., Lex = lóń-khrid-pa (long khrid pa). — ñes-dmigs (nyes dmigs) Mil. and elsewh., punishment. In the last three examples the etymological relationship is not quite evident.

a kind of evil demon, rarely mentioned Lex.; rmu-rgód (rmu rgod) wild, angry, passionate; a violent fellow, not safe to deal with Mil.; dmu-bló (dmu blo) a wild, irascible mind Sch.; hence dmus-byúń (dmus byung) terrifying, frightful Sch.; perh. also dmus-lóń (dmus long) blind, bodily blind, whilst lóń-ba (long ba) may be applied also to spiritual blindness Dzl., Glr. and elsewh., and dmu-čú (dmu chu) dropsy, esp. in the chest and in the belly Med.; dmu-skrán (dmu skran) Sch. an oedema, tumour filled with water.

I. vb. Cs. to curse, accurse, execrate, dmód-pa byéd-pa (dmod pa byed pa) id. Tar. 14. 17. — II. sbst. dmod-pa (dmod pa) Dzl., dmod (dmod), Glr. and elsewh., imprecation, execration, malediction; dmód-mo (dmod mo) id.; joined with °bór-ba ('bor ba), °dór-ba ('dor ba), °dzúg-pa ('dzug pa), smó-ba (smo ba): 1. to curse, to execrate, drań-sroń-gis dmod-pa bor-bai lo bču-yñis (drang srong gis dmod pa bor bai lo bcu gnyis) the twelve years on which a curse had been pronounced by the saint Dzl. 2. to swear, to confirm a treaty by an oath Glr. 3. to pronounce a prayer or conjuration, lha-la (lha la) to the deity Glr. 4. to affirm, e.g. to say '*kon-čóg śe*' (kon cog she) or the like. The word seems to be nearly related both to smód-pa (smod pa), and to smón-pa (smon pa), but, as expressly stated by the Lexx., is notsynon. with these verbs.

1. vb. to cut up, to cut into little pieces, meat at dinner Dzl., a punishment of hell Dzl. — II. sbst. hell, also sems-ćan-dmyál-ba (sems can dmyal ba); dmyál-bar °gró-ba (dmyal bar 'gro ba) to go to hell, dmyál-ba bčo-brgyád (dmyal ba bco brgyad)
